source: ESV Study Bible (Crossway) — Used under license
book_intro: The letter to the Hebrews was written to encourage Christians in a time of trial. It does so by focusing on the absolute supremacy and sufficiency of Jesus Christ. While God spoke in the past "many times and in many ways," he has now spoken to us "by his Son," Jesus Christ, who is the "exact imprint" of God's nature and who "upholds the universe by the word of his power" (1:1-3). Jesus accomplished complete salvation for all who trust in him (1:1-10:18). We dare not "neglect such a great salvation" (2:3; 5:12-6:20; 10:19-39). Rather, in our faith and in our everyday living, we should imitate the example of Christ and of those on the honor roll of faith (chs. 11-13). This letter, whose author is unknown, was probably written between a.d. 60 and 70.
chapter_intro: Chapter 5 defines the qualifications of a high priest — human solidarity with sinners, and divine appointment — and shows that Jesus meets both. He was 'in every respect tempted as we are,' and he was appointed by the Father with the oath of Ps 110:4: 'You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek.' The chapter closes with the sharpest rebuke in the letter: the readers, who by now ought to be teachers, have become dull of hearing and still need milk rather than solid food.

passages.title: The Qualifications of the Son as High Priest
passages.text: Every high priest is taken from humanity and appointed for humanity — both criteria are met in the Son who became flesh (ch. 2) and was appointed by the Father (Ps 2:7; 110:4). The vivid language of 'prayers and supplications, with loud cries and tears' in v.7 draws the reader into Gethsemane and Golgotha: the Son's priestly obedience was wrought through the most agonizing human experience.

passages.title: Warning: Spiritual Immaturity
passages.text: The transition from Christology to warning is abrupt — and intentional. The exposition of Melchizedek is 'hard to explain,' but the reason is not the material itself; it is the readers' dull spiritual hearing. Those who should be teachers need someone to teach them the ABCs again. The metaphor of milk vs. solid food contrasts immature faith with the trained discernment that comes through practice.

notes.text: deal gently — The Greek metriopathein means to moderate one's emotions: not indifferent to sin, but not crushingly harsh either. An angry or contemptuous high priest could not represent sinners before God.
Two OT citations — Ps 2:7 (Son) and Ps 110:4 (priest) — establish that Christ holds both royal and priestly offices, each by divine appointment, not human ambition.

he was heard because of his reverence — The Father did not spare Christ from death (Phil 2:8), but he heard the prayer of submission and raised him. Reverence (eulabeia) denotes godly fear, not dread.
made perfect — Not moral improvement (he was sinless), but completion of the qualifications needed for priesthood: he passed through suffering, death, and resurrection.
though by this time you ought to be teachers — Time in the faith does not automatically produce maturity. Spiritual growth requires ongoing engagement with the word.
